---
title: "Constitutional Compliance & Economic Growth"
subtitle: "A Python + Docker reproduction of Kucharska (2025)"
author: "Jan Wołyniak · Kinga Kucharska · Iwo Wiszejko"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    transition: slide
    slide-number: c/t
    chalkboard: false
    incremental: false
    width: 1280
    height: 800
    margin: 0.08
    code-overflow: wrap
    embed-resources: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    smaller: false
    scrollable: true
    logo: ""
    fig-align: center
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import HTML, Image, Markdown

REPO_ROOT = Path.cwd().parent if Path.cwd().name == "report" else Path.cwd()
OUTPUTS = REPO_ROOT / "outputs"
CROSS = OUTPUTS / "cross_section"
PANEL = OUTPUTS / "panel"


def load_csv(path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    if not path.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame({"_missing": [str(path)]})
    return pd.read_csv(path)


def small_table(df: pd.DataFrame, *, cols=None, max_rows: int = 8, decimals: int = 3):
    view = df if cols is None else df[[c for c in cols if c in df.columns]]
    numeric = view.select_dtypes(include="number").columns
    view = view.copy()
    view[numeric] = view[numeric].round(decimals)
    return HTML(
        view.head(max_rows).to_html(
            index=False, classes="table table-sm", border=0, justify="center"
        )
    )


def figure(path: Path):
    if not path.exists():
        return Markdown(f"*Missing figure: `{path}`*")
    return Image(filename=str(path))
```

# Draft notice {.center background-color="#fff3cd"}

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; max-width: 1000px; margin: auto;"}
**Status: template / MVP version.**

This deck is the **technical** option (option A — hybrid with the full
report). It is a starting structure: layout, ordering, and wording are a
first pass that will be revised once the team picks an option and we
rehearse the timing. The empirical numbers come from the regenerated
pipeline outputs and are correct; the presentation framing around them is
not final.
:::

# Part 1 — Setup and paper {.center}

::: {.notes}
Jan opens: introduce the team, the paper, and what we built. ~3 minutes.
:::

## The team and the split

|                  |                                                        |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Jan Wołyniak** | Project skeleton · Docker · orchestration · integration |
| **Kinga Kucharska** | Cross-sectional reproduction (OLS, diagnostics)     |
| **Iwo Wiszejko** | Panel reproduction (FE / RE / FD / robust SE)          |

Three contributors. Each commit lands under the author's own GitHub identity.

## The paper

**Kinga Kucharska**, *Constitutional Compliance: The Hidden Driver of
Economic Growth?*, **University of Warsaw, 2025** — file
`2400-LIC-FII.pdf` in the repo.

> What is the impact of constitutional compliance on economic growth, measured
> by GDP growth?

Two empirical designs:

- **Cross-section** — 2010–2019 averages, ~157 countries.
- **Panel** — 1990–2020 country-year, ~2,200 observations.

## The reproduction contract

We did not redesign the study. The contract that the Python code has to satisfy
is in `docs/reproduction_contract.md`:

- Same variables, same transformations.
- Same model families.
- Same target tables (paper Tables 3–5, appendix A1–A8).
- Tolerances: coefficients to `1e-6`, rounded table cells to displayed
  decimals.

The R workflow under `cross-section/` and `panel/` is the executable evidence
for what "the original result" means.

## What we shipped

- Python package `src/repro_research/` — loaders, transforms, models,
  diagnostics, validation, orchestration.
- One-command pipeline: `python scripts/run_all.py`.
- Docker image on Docker Hub:
  `janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii:phase6`.
- **This presentation is generated by the container.** The same image that
  runs the analysis also renders this deck and the full report.

# Part 2 — Cross-section {.center}

::: {.notes}
Kinga takes over: ~5 minutes. Walk through descriptives, OLS, institutional
alternatives, and verdict.
:::

## Cross-section: data

- Sample: 2010–2019 averages from `model_data4` (162 rows) and
  `model_data4_o` (157 rows; five outliers removed).
- Outliers dropped: **Congo Rep., Lesotho, Luxembourg, Malta, Singapore**.
- Loader normalises `country_name` / `country_code`, builds `log_GDPpc2015`,
  `log(cc_total + 2)`, and `terms_trade × trade`.
- Preferred regression sample: **122 complete observations** on the `re4_o`
  design.

## Spearman: GDP growth vs `cc_total`

```{python}
small_table(
    load_csv(CROSS / "spearman_growth_vs_cc_total.csv"),
    max_rows=8,
)
```

::: {.fragment}
Spearman correlation is **weak overall**, and structurally weaker in poorer
countries — already a clue that the cross-section will not pick up a
compliance effect.
:::

## Main OLS table (HC3 robust SE)

```{python}
df = load_csv(CROSS / "ols_main.csv")
df = df[df["model"] == "re4_o"][
    ["term", "coefficient", "robust_se_hc3", "p_value", "stars", "nobs", "r_squared"]
]
small_table(df, max_rows=10)
```

**`log(cc_total + 2)` ≈ −0.14, p ≈ 0.64, n.s.** Convergence and investment
are doing all the work.

## Institutional alternatives

Same regression, replace `cc_total` with a World Bank governance indicator:

```{python}
df = load_csv(CROSS / "ols_institutional_variants.csv")
mask = df["term"].isin(
    [
        "rule_of_law",
        "ctrl_of_corr",
        "gov_eff",
        "pol_stab",
        "reg_q",
        "voice_and_acc",
    ]
)
small_table(df.loc[mask, ["model", "term", "coefficient", "robust_se_hc3", "stars"]])
```

Almost all are **positive and significant**. The thesis's nuance: it is not
that institutions don't matter; it is that *the constitutional-compliance
index specifically* does not pick the signal up cross-sectionally.

## Diagnostics — match status

| Test           | R reference         | Python              |
| -------------- | ------------------- | ------------------- |
| RESET          | match               | match               |
| Shapiro-Wilk   | match               | match               |
| Breusch-Pagan  | match               | match               |
| Durbin-Watson  | match               | match               |
| VIFs           | match               | match               |
| Influence plots | best-effort styling | regenerated         |

All four diagnostic p-values reproduce the R export to four decimals — see
`outputs/cross_section/diagnostics.csv`.

## Cross-section verdict

- **Numerical:** every coefficient, robust SE, R², and diagnostic p-value
  matches the R reference at four decimals.
- **Economic:** the constitutional-compliance index does **not** predict
  growth cross-country, but other institutional-quality measures do.

# Part 3 — Panel {.center}

::: {.notes}
Iwo takes over: ~5 minutes. Walk through panel design, fixed-effects table,
Driscoll-Kraay, alternative components, verdict.
:::

## Why the panel changes the answer

Cross-section: between-country variation. The biggest cross-country growth
differences are driven by initial GDP, investment rate, and government size.
Compliance correlates with all of those.

Panel: **within-country** variation over 30 years. We absorb every
time-invariant country trait with a country fixed effect, and ask whether,
*for a given country*, an increase in compliance precedes higher GDP growth.

## Specification tests — pooled vs FE vs RE

```{python}
small_table(load_csv(PANEL / "specification_tests.csv"), max_rows=5)
```

- **LM** rejects pooled OLS in every block.
- **F test** for fixed effects rejects pooled OLS in every block.
- **Hausman** rejects random effects in favor of fixed effects in every
  block.

The publication tables use the FE estimator.

## Why we need robust SE

```{python}
small_table(load_csv(PANEL / "diagnostic_tests.csv"), max_rows=5)
```

Serial correlation, heteroskedasticity, **and** cross-sectional dependence
(Pesaran CD) are all present. We use **Driscoll-Kraay** standard errors,
which are designed exactly for this combination.

## Main fixed-effects table

```{python}
df = load_csv(PANEL / "fixed_effects_main.csv")
view = df[["term", "coefficient", "se_classical", "se_driscoll_kraay"]]
small_table(view, max_rows=10, decimals=3)
```

::: {.fragment}
**`log_cc_total = +1.74`, DK SE `0.29`** → roughly **1.7 pp of additional
annual GDP growth per log-point of compliance**, significant at any level.

R reference: +1.85 / DK SE 0.38. Same sign, same significance, same magnitude
to one decimal.
:::

## Alternative compliance components

```{python}
df = load_csv(PANEL / "fixed_effects_compliance_categories.csv")
mask = df["term"].isin(["log_cc_basic", "log_cc_civil", "log_cc_polit", "log_cc_prop"])
small_table(
    df.loc[mask, ["compliance_variant", "term", "coefficient", "se_driscoll_kraay"]],
    max_rows=8,
)
```

| Component  | Sign | Significant at 5% |
| ---------- | :--: | :---------------: |
| `cc_basic` |  +   |        yes        |
| `cc_civil` |  +   |        yes        |
| `cc_polit` |  +   |        no         |
| `cc_prop`  |  +   |        yes        |

Same pattern as the paper. The compliance effect is carried by **basic
rights, civil liberties, and property rights**; political-organisation rights
move the right way but not significantly.

## Panel verdict

- Coefficients match R to one decimal across all blocks.
- DK SE is consistently *smaller* in Python — known finite-sample
  correction difference between `linearmodels` / local SCC and R
  `sandwich::vcovSCC`. Documented in
  `docs/panel_reproduction_results.md`.
- Substantive conclusion is identical: **within countries, compliance pays
  off in growth**.

# Part 4 — Reproducibility {.center}

::: {.notes}
Jan again: ~4 minutes. Show the pipeline, the Docker image, the validation.
This is where we run the live command.
:::

## The pipeline

`python scripts/run_all.py` runs:

1. Source audit (`scripts/audit_phase0.py`)
2. Data inventory (`scripts/inventory_data.py`)
3. Cross-section prep + analysis
4. Panel prep + analysis
5. Manifest validation

Each step appends a line to `outputs/logs/run_all_summary.txt`. A clean run
ends with every step `ok`.

## The Docker image

```text
janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii:phase6
```

- Base: `python:3.11-slim`
- Pinned scientific stack: `pandas`, `numpy`, `scipy`, `statsmodels`,
  `linearmodels`, `pyreadr`, `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, …
- **Quarto CLI** included — same image that runs the analysis renders this
  deck.
- Default command: `python scripts/generate_report.py` → full pipeline +
  rendered report + slides under `outputs/report/`.

## The one reviewer command

```bash
docker pull janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii:phase6

docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd)/outputs:/app/outputs" \
  janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii:phase6
```

After that:

- `outputs/report/report.html` — the full report.
- `outputs/report/slides.html` — these slides.
- `outputs/cross_section/`, `outputs/panel/` — every table and figure.
- `outputs/logs/run_all_summary.txt` — per-script status.

## Validation

`scripts/validate_project.py --generated-outputs` checks every expected
artifact is present and non-empty. The container runs it before exiting; if
anything is missing, the container exits non-zero.

```text
[ok] outputs/cross_section/descriptive_statistics.csv
[ok] outputs/cross_section/ols_main.csv
[ok] outputs/panel/fixed_effects_main.csv
[ok] outputs/panel/specification_tests.csv
[ok] outputs/report/report.html
[ok] outputs/report/slides.html
…
```

## Live demo {.smaller}

What we show next, in this order:

1. `docker pull …:phase6` on the lab laptop (or pre-pulled).
2. `docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/outputs:/app/outputs …:phase6`.
3. Open `outputs/report/report.html` — scroll through the cross-section and
   panel sections.
4. Open `outputs/logs/run_all_summary.txt` — show every step `ok`.
5. Open `outputs/panel/fixed_effects_main.csv` — read the +1.74 / 0.29 row.

If the lab network can't pull the image, we fall back to the pre-rendered
`outputs/report/report.html` in the cloned repo.

# Part 5 — Wrap-up {.center}

## Honest limitations

- **HTML / corrplot styling** is best-effort, not pixel-exact.
- **Driscoll-Kraay SE** uses the SCC formulation; magnitudes differ from R
  `sandwich::vcovSCC` by a finite-sample correction.
- **`plm::pbgtest`** has no exact Python twin — substituted with a labelled
  Breusch-Godfrey panel proxy.
- **AI assistance** (OpenAI Codex / GPT-5-class) was used for boilerplate and
  refactoring; every model spec and result was human-reviewed. Full
  disclosure in `docs/ai_disclosure.md`.

## What we want you to take away

1. **The paper's substantive conclusion holds under independent Python
   reproduction.** Cross-section: no compliance effect. Panel: +1.74
   percentage points of growth per log-point, robust to DK SE.
2. **The reproduction is one command.** `docker pull … && docker run …`
   regenerates every table, every figure, this report, and these slides.
3. **The match status is documented.** Every table and figure has an
   explicit "match / partial / divergent" verdict in `docs/reproduction_results.md`.

## Thank you {.center}

Repository: <https://github.com/janwolyniak/Reproducible_Research>

Docker image: `janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii:phase6`

Questions?
